topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the attic was thick, pressing against my eardrums. Dust motes danced in the single shaft of sunlight piercing the gloom, illuminating towers of old textbooks. I hadn't meant to come up here, but a strange compulsion had pulled me toward the forgotten corners of the house. Then I saw it: a neatly labeled box, tucked behind a stack of yellowed yearbooks. Inside, nestled amongst brittle paper, were every single assignment from my entire academic career. My hands started to tremble as I lifted a crumpled worksheet from freshman year. It was overwhelming. The sheer volume of obligation, all preserved, felt like a physical weight settling onto my chest. I backed away slowly, the sound of my own breathing suddenly loud in the stillness.

[story 2]

My stomach churned with a nervous energy that made my palms slick. I was supposed to be cleaning out the garage, but instead, I found the old filing cabinet in the spare room. Behind a loose panel, I discovered it—a trove. Every homework sheet, every test paper, every essay, meticulously saved. A cold prickle ran down my spine. I picked up a geometry proof from sophomore year. The ink seemed to vibrate under my fingertips. I kept glancing toward the hallway, half-expecting someone to walk in, to notice this secret. I quickly shoved the box back into its hiding spot, my heart hammering a frantic rhythm against my ribs, desperate to get out of that suffocating space.

[story 3]

I paced the length of the kitchen floor, the hardwood protesting beneath my bare feet. Every shadow seemed too deep, every creak of the house amplified into a sharp sound. It started when I found the old cedar chest in the basement. It wasn't what I expected, but as I lifted the lid, the sight of the organized chaos inside—hundreds of papers, meticulously cataloged—stopped me dead. It was my mother’s secret archive. A profound sense of unease washed over me, a tightening in my throat. I didn't touch anything, just stared at the sheer volume of evidence, feeling suddenly exposed, as if every moment of my academic life was being scrutinized. I needed to leave. Now.
